preamble: The shift from the velar stop [k] to the alveolar stop [t] is a classic example of 'velar fronting'. It is a nearly universal phenomenon in child language acquisition, where speakers substitute front-of-mouth sounds (which are easier to articulate and observe) for back-of-mouth sounds. It also occurs occasionally as a permanent historical dialect shift.

examples.note: Developmental substitution replacing the velar closure with an alveolar closure.
Historical fronting of the velar stop towards the alveolar/palatal ridge.
